title: Alnylam — AHP — Synthesis
html:
Cross-Study Synthesis: GIVLAARI / Acute Hepatic Porphyria

Scope: Patterns across ZoomRx deliverables -- Global ATU Wave 3-4, US ATU ad-hoc, US PET Q2 2023, Global ATU 2024 KO, US PET Wave 7 KO Last updated: April 2026

1. Governing Insight

GIVLAARI's commercial trajectory is fundamentally constrained not by competition but by the size of the diagnosed patient pool and by payer access barriers. The ZoomRx data consistently shows that the brand wins once a patient is identified and put on therapy, but the market's ceiling is set by diagnostic delay (up to 15 years) and cost/coverage restrictions that keep ~45% of diagnosed patients in Brazil and a meaningful share in the US off consistent treatment. The most productive strategic lever for Alnylam is not message optimization or competitive positioning -- it is expanding who gets diagnosed and who gets access.

2. Cross-Study Pattern: Disease Perception Is Shifting -- and It Matters

Pattern across ATU Waves 3 and 4 (2023-2024):

The most consequential shift in the ZoomRx data is the steady reframing of AHP from an episodic attack disease to a chronic condition with sustained biochemical toxicity.

  • US (W4, n=73): Majority of HCPs believe AHP is predominantly a chronic condition. Primary treatment objective has shifted to preventing long-term complications from elevated ALA/PBG, not just preventing attacks. This is statistically significant relative to prior waves.
  • Germany (W4, n=25): Treatment focus shifted from purely LTC prevention (W3) to a balanced approach encompassing both attack prevention and LTC reduction (W4) -- suggesting an equilibrium has been reached.
  • Brazil (W4, n=15): The outlier. HCPs still prioritize attack prevention over LTC management. Less likely to consider an AHP event that does not result in medical intervention as a true attack. This is a structural perception gap, not a lag.

Why this matters for GIVLAARI: The shift toward chronicity supports broader GIVLAARI use -- beyond recurrent attackers (Patient Type 1) into chronic symptom patients (Type 4) and even biochemically active patients (Type 5). In the US, the top driver for GIVLAARI prescription is now "improvement in chronic symptoms" (59%), not attack reduction. This is a fundamental value proposition shift that Alnylam's messaging should anchor on -- and ZoomRx should track whether the gap between US and Brazil narrows over time.

3. Cross-Study Pattern: The HCP Adoption Ladder Is Clear -- Conversion Requires Different Levers at Each Rung

Convergent finding from US ATU (W4) and PET (Q2 2023):

ZoomRx's HCP segmentation (Enthusiasts, Trialists, Converters, Inexperienced) reveals that adoption barriers are not uniform:

Segment What they need ATU evidence PET evidence
Enthusiasts (11%) Payer support to maintain share Payer coverage/inclusion criteria is top barrier Reps are well-received; high NPS
Trialists (21%) OOP cost relief High OOP costs most notable barrier Respond well to clinical data messaging
Converters (22%) Efficacy differentiation vs. hemin Perceive GIVLAARI efficacy as similar/lower than hemin Need compelling trial-data-led conversations (% with 0 attacks)
Inexperienced (47%) Education + payer pathway Patient not meeting inclusion criteria limits adoption Respond to MOA messaging; low engagement with brand

Key synthesis: The largest untapped segment (Inexperienced, 47%) defaults to hemin-as-needed not because they reject GIVLAARI but because their patients don't meet payer-driven attack thresholds. This is a market access problem masquerading as an adoption problem. The PET data confirms that when reps reach these HCPs, the interactions are effective -- but they happen less frequently.

The Converter segment (22%) is the one where messaging matters most. These HCPs have the patients (high AHP load) but question whether GIVLAARI is truly better than hemin. The PET shows that the "50% attack-free vs. 17% placebo" message (GIV03) and the "70% reduction in hemin use" message (GIV08) are the most effective tools -- but the "daily worst pain" (GIV11) and "analgesic use" (GIV12) messages, despite being highly motivating, are recalled far below industry standards. There is a delivery gap that the field force can fix.

4. Cross-Study Pattern: Global Heterogeneity Is Structural, Not Just Cultural

Convergent finding from Global ATU Wave 4 across US, Germany, Brazil:

The three markets are not at different stages of the same journey -- they face fundamentally different structural challenges:

Dimension US Germany Brazil
Primary barrier Payer access (OOP, inclusion criteria) Patient refusal, administration burden Cost/payer restrictions (45% of patients untreated)
Disease framing Chronic condition Chronic condition Episodic attack disease
GIVLAARI preference driver Chronic symptom improvement Attack reduction + targeted MOA Attack reduction
Age sensitivity No age exclusion Age matters (prefer younger) Age matters (prefer younger)
Proximity to care Moderate influence Limited influence Significant influence (40%)
Growth trajectory +8% expected share growth +9% expected share growth (highest GIVLAARI share already) Flat (~26%)
HCP practice setting 66% community 68% academic 66% community

Implication for study design: Future ATU waves should not use identical strategic frameworks for all three markets. The US story is about HCP conversion and messaging; the Germany story is about sustaining and growing already-high adoption; the Brazil story is about structural access and disease education.

5. Cross-Study Pattern: Promotional Effectiveness Is High -- The Ceiling Is Not Messaging

From PET Q2 2023 (US):

  • All GIVLAARI messages rated above rare disease industry average for effectiveness
  • Over 50% of sales calls are "high-impact details"
  • Reps rated above industry standards on all performance attributes
  • NPS trending upward (more promoters in Q2 2023 vs. Q4 2022)
  • In-person detailing is preferred and increasing

From ATU Wave 4 (US):

  • Top GIVLAARI driver is chronic symptom efficacy (59%) -- aligned with the disease perception shift
  • Top barriers are OOP costs (56%) and payer coverage (53%) -- not messaging deficits

Synthesis: The rep force is performing well. Messages are effective. The problem is not what reps say -- it is that many potential patients are never diagnosed, and among those who are, cost/coverage blocks treatment. Promotional investment has diminishing returns unless paired with market access innovation and disease awareness campaigns.

6. Cross-Study Pattern: Hemin's Role Is Evolving, Not Disappearing

Across ATU and PET data:

  • PANHEMATIN (as-needed) retains a significant role, especially for acute attack management
  • In the US, GIVLAARI is gaining share primarily from prophylactic PANHEMATIN users and treatment-naive patients -- not from as-needed hemin
  • In Brazil, hemin-as-needed has a higher patient share than GIVLAARI, and cost barriers make this unlikely to change without structural intervention
  • In Germany, GIVLAARI already has the highest patient share -- hemin is being displaced
  • 63% of current US GIVLAARI patients were switched from prophylactic PANHEMATIN, mostly Type 1 (recurrent attackers)
  • GIVLAARI satisfaction scores are high and improving; prophylactic PANHEMATIN satisfaction is lower

Implication: Hemin-as-needed is not a competitor to GIVLAARI -- it is a complement for acute rescue. The real competitor is "no treatment" (treatment-naive patients). Future ATU waves should track the treatment-naive pool size and reasons more closely.

7. Cross-Study Pattern: The "Chronic Symptom" Frontier Is the Growth Opportunity

Convergent evidence across all sources:

  • Patient Type 4 (chronic symptoms, no attacks in 2 years) is the fastest-growing GIVLAARI patient segment in the US
  • US HCPs' top GIVLAARI treatment goal is "improve chronic symptoms" -- ahead of "prevent attacks" in some segments
  • GIVLAARI is notably preferred for patients developing chronic pain requiring weekly analgesics (63% of US HCPs, 56% of German HCPs)
  • Peripheral neuropathy is a particularly strong GIVLAARI preference trigger across regions
  • The "chronic symptom" framing also supports treatment of patients who don't meet the "4+ attacks" payer threshold

Strategic opportunity: Alnylam should position GIVLAARI not just as attack prevention but as chronic disease management -- reducing the daily burden of AHP beyond attack frequency. This framing:

  1. Expands the treatable population (Types 4 and 5)
  2. Aligns with the evolving HCP belief system
  3. May unlock payer pathways that don't require attack-count thresholds
  4. Differentiates more strongly from hemin (which only addresses acute attacks)

8. Emerging Signal: Off-Label Dosing and Unmet Needs

From US ATU Wave 4:

  • 14% of US HCPs reported administering GIVLAARI on a different dosage than recommended, mostly for patients with 4+ attacks or severe history
  • 84% of HCPs achieve intended dosing schedule -- meaning 16% face adherence challenges
  • ZoomRx has reported off-label use cases to the appropriate regulatory agency

Implication: There may be a subset of patients for whom monthly dosing is insufficient. This is worth tracking -- it could signal opportunity for a dosing regimen optimization study or, if Alnylam pursues it, a label update.

9. Open Questions for Future ZoomRx Work

  1. Diagnostic journey tracking: No ZoomRx study currently measures the patient journey from symptom onset to AHP diagnosis. This is the #1 commercial bottleneck and should be explored -- potentially via a patient-reported outcomes study or HCP retrospective.

  2. Treatment-naive deep dive: What keeps diagnosed, untreated patients off therapy? Is it HCP reluctance, patient refusal, payer denial, or severity perception? The current ATU captures this superficially but could go deeper.

  3. Chronic symptom outcomes data: As GIVLAARI's value proposition shifts toward chronic symptom management, ZoomRx should explore whether HCPs have real-world evidence (beyond trial data) that GIVLAARI improves chronic symptoms. This could inform Alnylam's RWE strategy.

  4. PET refresh needed: The most recent PET data is from Q2 2023. Given the disease perception shifts captured in ATU Wave 4 (fielded Sep-Oct 2024), a refreshed PET would reveal whether rep messaging has adapted to the chronicity narrative.

  5. Brazil structural access study: The ~45% of diagnosed patients in Brazil not on consistent treatment represents a fundamentally different problem than what the ATU is designed to measure. A dedicated access/payer study for Brazil could be valuable.
